steps.title: Condition for infinitely many solutions
steps.content: A system of two linear equations has infinitely many solutions only when both equations describe the same line. So the second equation must be algebraically equivalent to $y=6x+3$.

steps.title: Simplify each candidate
steps.content:
Reduce each choice to slope-intercept form and compare to $y=6x+3$:
  • A) $y=2(6x)+3=12x+3$. Slope $12 eq 6$, so this is a different line.
  • B) $y=2(6x+3)=12x+6$. Slope $12 eq 6$, so this is a different line.
  • C) $2y=2(6x)+3=12x+3$, so $y=6x+ rac{3}{2}$. The intercept $ rac{3}{2} eq 3$, so this is a different line.
  • D) $2y=2(6x+3)=12x+6$, so $y=6x+3$. This is identical to the original equation, so the two equations describe the same line and the system has infinitely many solutions. That matches choice D — D is correct.
